asset_limits.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

regional_variations.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

waitlist.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

comparisons.name: Medicaid Buy-In for Children (MBIC) / Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualifying Individual (QI)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: MBIC income scales by household size (300% FPL); premiums vary by income/insurance; QI capped enrollment; dual MBIC (kids) vs Medicare help (elderly); disability=SSI criteria key
gotchas: MBIC premium required (sliding scale—not free; based on AGI, family size, employer insurance).
Income up to 300% FPL only—many assume 'too high' disqualifies entirely.
QI: Waitlist/annual funding limits (not guaranteed).
QMB/SLMB/QI assets exclude home/car but count cash—providers can't bill QMB patients.
Must meet SSI-level disability (not just diagnosis).
Children up to 18 only (transition to adult Buy-In at 19 if working).
Forms computer-only; confirm receipt post-submit.

comparisons.name: STAR+PLUS

diffs.found: Managed care Medicaid including acute care (physician visits, hospital, pharmacy, dental, vision, NEMT), long-term services (service coordination, nursing, therapies), and HCBS waiver for those qualifying: Day Activity & Health Services (DAHS, up to 5 days/week Mon-Fri for physical/mental/medical/social needs), Personal Attendant Services (PAS), minor home modifications, specialized medical equipment, respite, transition services. Nursing facility residents get room/board, supplies, rehab. Extra plan-specific perks (e.g., Weight Watchers subscription, camp allowances, asthma supplies) vary by MCO.[3][5][6]

novelFields.value: Assets limited to $2,000 (countable resources). Exempt: primary home if equity ≤ $730,000 and applicant lives there or intends to return, or if spouse/child under 21/disabled child lives there; one vehicle; personal belongings; burial plots; life insurance up to $1,500 face value.[1][4]
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Different MCOs/providers by region (e.g., Community First central TX, Molina, Superior, Wellpoint); some extras limited to counties (e.g., Weight Watchers in Dallas/Hidalgo); wait times vary regionally
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Long interest lists (many years wait for HCBS invitation); some exceptions exist
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Income proof (pay stubs, SSI award letters)
Physician certification for level of care
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Long interest list wait for HCBS waiver; MCO-specific in regions; fixed individual income/asset limits (no household scaling); NFLOC medical tier required
gotchas: Must join interest list first; years-long wait for HCBS (not immediate)
Income/asset limits apply individually even for couples; home equity cap $730,000
Requires NFLOC (nursing home level need); not just low income/age
Managed by MCOs—select carefully as benefits/extras vary by plan/region
Cannot be in another waiver; nursing home residents may transition out
Applicant pays applied income toward nursing facility if applicable

comparisons.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Only available in limited Texas regions via specific providers like Bienvivir; no direct income/asset test for PACE but tied to Medicaid financials for full benefits; requires nursing home certification while allowing community living
gotchas: Limited geographic availability in Texas—not statewide, must live in specific provider service area[1][2]
No Medicare Advantage or hospice enrollment allowed[2]
State certification for nursing home level care required, involves assessment[1][4]
Most participants dually eligible, but private pay possible if not Medicaid-qualified[2][3]
Voluntary enrollment; approval by Texas state after provider assistance[1]

comparisons.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Seniors 60+ simplified: net income only, no gross test; benefits scale by household size; medical/shelter deductions key for elderly; Texas-specific asset limit and HHSC administration.
gotchas: Seniors only need **net income test** (not gross)—deduct medical/shelter costs to qualify[1][3].
Own home/savings? Often still eligible (exempt assets)[1].
Include **all household members** who share food, even non-eligible[1].
Social Security/pensions **count as income**[1][3].
Texas has **asset test** ($2k); not waived like some states[4].
Work rules exempt seniors 60+, but verify if under 60[2][5].
2025 changes: Updated work/non-citizen rules via One Big Beautiful Bill Act[3][5].

comparisons.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Administered regionally via 254 county subrecipients (CAAs/CEAP); income by household size; no asset test; benefits vary by fuel type, crisis status, and local funding; waitlists and slight guideline variations by area.
gotchas: Primarily delivered as CEAP in Texas (integrates LIHEAP funds); contact local CAA, not statewide office.
Household includes all at address sharing utility bill (unlike SNAP).
Funding limited by federal availability; waitlists common.
Benefits paid directly to utility, not applicant.
Priorities for elderly/disabled/crisis, but open to all qualifying.[1][2][4]

comparisons.name: Weatherization Assistance Program

diffs.found: Energy audit of home efficiency (identifies air leaks, inefficient appliances). Installation of weatherization measures including caulking, weather-stripping, ceiling/wall/floor insulation, patching building envelope holes, duct work, tune-up/repair/replacement of heating/cooling systems. Must meet specific energy-savings goals per DOE standards. No fixed dollar amount or hours; services based on audit results. Education on energy savings. Does not typically replace full HVAC systems[6][4][3].

novelFields.value: No asset limits mentioned across sources.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Yes: different subrecipients/providers per region with unique applications, phones, addresses, forms, home type rules (e.g., mobile homes excluded by some[3], allowed by others[5]), additional requirements (e.g., Austin Energy customer, property value limits[5]), wait times/funding vary[4][6]. Full subrecipient list via TDHCA.
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Common due to funding limits; prioritization matrix used (e.g., points for elderly, disabled, children under 6); strive for timely service but patience requested[4].
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of income for all household members 18+ (last 30 days): check stubs, SSI/SSDI/SSA award letters, unemployment docs, pension/retirement letters, workers comp, TANF; notarized Declaration if no income. Bank statements/W2s/tax docs not accepted[1][2][4][7].
Proof of citizenship/immigration for all household members: birth certificate, naturalization/citizenship certificate, US passport[1].
Proof of ID for all 18+: driver's license, state ID, passport, military ID, permanent residency card[1].
Household info: names, DOB for all members[7].
Utility bills: current electric/gas/propane with 12-month history[7].
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Administered via ~30 regional subrecipients with unique apps/forms/contacts; 200% FPL income test; priority tiers by vulnerability; services audit-driven, not fixed amount; county-restricted providers; mobile home eligibility varies; strict 30-day income docs only.
gotchas: Must contact correct regional subrecipient, not state directly; statewide but hyper-local[6].
Income proof strictly last 30 days annualized, no bank statements/W2s; notarized zero-income declaration required[1][7].
Citizenship/qualified alien verification for EVERY household member, including children[4][1].
Home must pass energy audit; no service if not needed. Mobile homes often excluded[3][6].
Prioritization tiers (elderly/disabled/young kids first); long waitlists common[4].
Renter needs landlord permission; owners may need financial commitment info[1][5].
No recent prior weatherization (e.g., last 10 years in some areas)[5].
Varies by utility/provider in some regions (e.g., Austin Energy customers only)[5].

comparisons.name: Texas State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: no income/asset test; counseling service only, not benefits; statewide via local networks; Texas-specific name HICAP; volunteer-driven with national oversight by ACL[1][2][4][7]
gotchas: Not a direct healthcare or financial aid program—provides counseling only, not payments or services; in Texas called Health Information, Counseling, and Advocacy Program (HICAP); free but relies on trained volunteers/staff, so availability may depend on local capacity; often confused with Medicare Savings Programs it helps apply for[1][3][6][7]
No enrollment wait or caps, but peak times (e.g., Open Enrollment) may have higher demand[2]

comparisons.name: Meals on Wheels

diffs.found: Home-delivered nutritious, often medically-tailored meals (typically 1 hot meal per weekday; some offer weekend frozen meals). Includes safety check and brief social contact by volunteer deliverers; case management for holistic needs assessment.[1][2][4]

novelFields.value: No asset limits mentioned across programs.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Operated by independent local providers with slight differences in age/disability rules, priority groups, delivery details, and short-term options; must contact local program.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

novelFields.value: Possible in some programs, leading to longer processing.[1]
novelFields.note: Has waitlist info — our model has no wait time field

value: Proof of age, residence, medical info/dietary restrictions (sometimes); health problems, DOB, SSN, doctor's info, emergency contacts (requested at referral).[1][4]
Financial docs rare, as income not a factor.[1]
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Decentralized local providers with no statewide uniformity; no income/asset tests; service area and minor eligibility nuances vary by county/region; find via local Area Agency on Aging or Meals on Wheels America search.
gotchas: Not a single statewide program—must find and contact the specific local provider for your area; eligibility/geography strictly local.[1][2][3][6]
Homebound status key: those who can easily leave home or have cooking help may not qualify.[1][4]
Car ownership or non-homebound status may disqualify.[1]
Voluntary contributions expected but not required; some trial meals require payment if skipping full assessment.[6]
Short-term service available for recovery but limited (e.g., 6 weeks).[3]

comparisons.name: Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Capped by nursing facility payment rate; priority-based waitlist; integrated with STAR+PLUS managed care; financial eligibility via standard Medicaid + waiver-specific NFLOC; no fixed service hours/dollars, plan individualized.
gotchas: Must choose waiver over nursing facility (informed choice required; cannot dual-enroll).
Not automatic Medicaid; separate financial/functional eligibility checks.
Waitlists can be years long; apply to interest list early even if not ready.
Income over SSI may still qualify via waiver rules (up to 300% SSI) but requires spend-down.
Home equity limit $730,000; estate recovery may apply post-death.
Overlaps with STAR+PLUS HCBS; some STAR+PLUS enrollees excluded if under 1115 waiver.
Services capped by nursing facility cost; not unlimited.

comparisons.name: Community Caregiver Support Program
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: No dedicated Texas 'Community Caregiver Support Program' found; data fragmented across CCAD (non-Medicaid in-home aid), STAR+PLUS (Medicaid waiver), and possible confusion with VA program; county-level ADRC delivery with income/functional tests
gotchas: Program name may refer to VA national program, not Texas-specific; families should verify with Texas HHS as results point to CCAD or STAR+PLUS instead[1][2][6]
Eligibility often tied to not receiving duplicate Medicaid services; income/assets adjusted annually[2]
Caregiver support under Texas Admin Code requires meeting broader criteria in §700.1003, details unavailable here[5]
comparisons.officialSource: No primary .gov URL for exact 'Community Caregiver Support Program'; see Texas HHS for CCAD/CCSE: implied via ADRC contacts[2]

comparisons.name: Legal Aid for Seniors through AAAs
comparisons.dataShapeNotes: Decentralized across 28 regional AAAs covering all counties; no fixed income/asset tests but need-based prioritization; focuses on referrals to civil legal aid with separate eligibility; services free with ombudsman/benefit counseling core[3][4]
gotchas: Not direct legal representation; primarily counseling, ombudsman advocacy, and referrals to low-income legal aid orgs like Legal Aid of NorthWest Texas which have stricter 125-200% FPL income limits[1][2][4]
Legal aid referrals require separate low-income eligibility (e.g., below 125-200% Federal Poverty Guidelines, U.S. citizen/LPR)[1][2]
Prioritization for highest need (e.g., low-income minorities, rural, isolated, high nutritional risk); others may get referrals only[3][4]
Civil matters only; no criminal/personal injury[1]
Contact local AAA first, not statewide number[4][5]

comparisons.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program

novelFields.value: No asset limits; no financial eligibility requirements apply.
novelFields.note: Our model has no asset limit fields

novelFields.value: Administered locally by Area Agencies on Aging or independent councils (e.g., H-GAC in Harris County[1][3], Texoma Council of Governments[4], CAPCOG[9], AAACP[6]); availability depends on local volunteer/staff presence but services are free and confidential statewide.
novelFields.note: Program varies by region — our model doesn't capture this

value: No documents needed to initiate contact; provide details of the resident's situation, facility name, and specific concerns.
novelFields.note: Has document checklist — our model doesn't store per-program documents

comparisons.dataShapeNotes: no income test; open to all LTC residents statewide via local independent councils; advocacy-focused, not benefits/entitlements; volunteer-driven with regional administration
gotchas: Not a service provider or eligibility-determining program—purely advocacy for existing LTC residents, not for admission or financial aid.[5][9]
Volunteers/staff must be independent with no conflicts (e.g., no facility employment or Medicaid eligibility roles).[2]
Confidentiality applies to all complaints.[5]
Does not cover non-LTC settings or developmental disability state centers.[7]
People mistake it for direct care/funding; it's complaint resolution and rights protection only.
